posts.author: mlaflamm

tickets.summary: Detect ambiguous table names
tickets.description:
When no schema is specified, it's possible for DbUnit 
to get column information from multiple tables \(from 
different schemas\).  In this case, the SQL that is 
created won't work.

DbUnit could detect the case where it is getting 
columns from multiple schemas.  It could give some 
sort of &quot;Ambiguous Table Name&quot; error to alert the user 
to the root problem.  When a method like 
DatabaseTableMetaData.getColumns retrieves a list of 
column names, it could look at the schema name for 
each to make sure they're all the same.

tickets.summary: UseBatch system property
tickets.description:
Please add a system property that that indicates that 
batch inserts should NOT be performed, even if the 
driver says that it &quot;supports&quot; them.

This flag is necessary to work around a defect in the 
DB2 7.1 JDBC driver. The driver blows up with an 
access violation if you attempt to perform batch 
inserts on a column that contains binary data. For 
example, if you have a simple table like:

CREATE TABLE test \(
id CHAR\(16\) FOR BIT DATA NOT NULL,
scope VARCHAR\(255\) NOT NULL,
CONSTRAINT pk\_test PRIMARY KEY \(id,scope\)\)

Everything works fine if individual inserts are 
performed using a PreparedStatement. Only batch 
inserts fail. Batch inserts work fine on non binary 
data.

dbunit will use batch updates if the driver supports 
it. This is a problem for me due the DB2 bug.

posts.author: oman002

tickets.summary: Share transaction with InsertIdentityOp.
tickets.description:
Please change InsertIdentityOperation to allow it to share 
an existing transaction rather than assume that autocommit 
is on.

One database testing technique is to do the following:
1\) Begin a transaction
2\) Set up test data \(with DbUnit\!\)
3\) Call the tested method
4\) Verify the results \(with DbUnit\!\)
5\) Rollback the transaction

A rollback ensures that all data changes are correctly 
removed even if the tested method made unexpected data 
changes.  And it can be faster than deleting the records.  
\(For example, when a table has many unindexed foreign 
keys pointing to it.\)

This technique cannot currently be used with 
InsertIdentityOperation because this operation expects to 
control the transaction itself.  It would be nice if there was a 
switch of some sort that would cause it to use the existing 
transaction without comitting or changing the autocommit 
flag.

You can do that using the CompositeTable class like this: 

Assertion.assertEquals\(expected, 
new CompositeTable\(expected.getTableMetaData\(\), actual\)\);

This is very important to not invert Actual and Expected 
tables. The Actual table is the database table snapshot you 
want to verify against the Expected table. As its name imply, 
the Expected table contains the expectation values that 
usually comes from an XML dataset or can be created in 
memory. 

The Actual table MUST contain at least ALL the Expected 
table columns. Extra columns can exist in the Actual table 
but not in the Expected one.

tickets.summary: Validate XML against its DTD
tickets.description:
If an invalid xml file is given to dbunit, this is ignored.
dbunit looks on DTD and assumes that the xml file
contains correct columns.

I had a problem with that when my DTD has fewer columns
than the actual XML. This meant that those columns
values were not inserted into the table, and no error
reported.
It took a lot of time until I realized what is happening.

The problems seems to be because Electric XML is a
non-validating parser.
The solution is to use a validating parser \(like Xerces
or whatever\).

tickets.summary: allow to ignore specified columns in Assertions class
tickets.description:
There are some scenarios when data is generated by 
some custom code \(i.e. JDBC or EJB BMP code\) and to 
validate such data with DBUnit it is necessary to ignore 
some columns with autogenerated numbers. The 
simplest implementation is to use FlatXMLDataSet, but 
values for the generated columns \(usually primary keys\) 
should be ignored.

I suggest to introduce a new method in the Assertion 
class which will ignore data in the specified columns.

Sure.
What is meant with &quot;having table names that 
contain keys&quot;?
Easiest is to show by an example.
Just look in class org.dbunit.database.DatabaseDataSet.
There is a method 
private Map getTableMap\(\) throws DataSetException \{..\}
In this method there are the statements:
// prevent table name conflict
if \(tableMap.containsKey\(tableName\)\)
\{
throw new AmbiguousTableNameException
\(tableName\); //THIS LINE HAS BEEN COMMENTED OUT\!
\}

The passus &quot;that contains keys&quot; is an offspring of the method 
name containsKey\(..\) used here. So a database table that 
contains a substring in its name, that is equal to a keyword of 
the database is seen as invalid right now.

As remarked in the above shown lines of code, I just 
commented out one line to fit our needs. More elegent would 
be to allow configuration in this matter.

Best regards

Klaus

PS: I do like your framework and have several ideas of 
improvements. Our project is at a point where I could make 
some extensions to dbUnit. What do you think?
posts.subject: #17 Allow table names with conflict in DatabaseDataSet
posts.author: klausikm

posts.text:
Logged In: YES 
user\_id=466344

You seem to believe that your problem is caused by a table 
name that is a substring of another table name. This cannot 
be the case as the table name must be \*exactly\* equals 
\(even by case\). 

Your problem is more likely caused by a table that exists in 
more than one schema. This is a FAQ. You can get more info 
at:
http://www.dbunit.org/faq.html\#AmbiguousTableNameExceptio
n

I think this is unsafe to allow DbUnit accessing multiple tables 
with the same name. Import and export becomes 
unpredictable in such environment.

\---

Don't hesitate to post your DbUnit extension suggestions. I 
cannot guarantee they will end up in DbUnit exactly as you 
thought or coded them. Also be aware that the current CVS 
version is a lot different from the 1.5.1 distribution. The best 
place to discuss about this is in the developer \(or even in the 
user\) mailing list.

\(..\) ist called. This one calls dataSet.getTables\(\)
But when in the end dataSet.getTableMap\(\) is called 
indirectly and internally.
getTableMap\(\) gets all table names from the database that 
are not system tables \(see javadoc of the method\).
But why is is from interest for me, If I want to write a specific 
dataSet to an XML file, if there is a conflict with a table name 
somewhere in the database ?
This is what I don' understand.

posts.text: ColumnsExcludedTable and ColumnsExcludedTableMetaData
posts.subject: #23 SOLUTION: Table Decorator to ignore columns
posts.author: mkienenb

posts.text:
Logged In: YES 
user\_id=466344

Like I wrote in \[947909\], I created the IColumnFilter interface 
a few weeks ago having in mind to use it in a decorator 
similar to your proposal. 
http://cvs.sourceforge.net/viewcvs.py/dbunit/dbunit/src/java
/org/dbunit/dataset/filter/IColumnFilter.java

I now have created a first decorator, FilteredTableMetaData, 
using this interface. Here is an example that excludes all 
columns with prefix "PK" and suffix "TIME":

DefaultColumnFilter columnFilter = new DefaultColumnFilter\(\);
columnFilter.excludeColumn\("PK\*"\);
columnFilter.excludeColumn\("\*TIME"\);

FilteredTableMetaData metaData = new FilteredTableMetaData
\(
table.getTableMetaData\(\), columnFilter\);
table = new CompositeTable\(metaData, table\);

posts.author: mlaflamm

tickets.summary: SOLUTION: Table Decorator to ignore columns
tickets.description:
One of the most requested items is the ability to 
selectively ignore certain table columns, like primary 
keys and record creation dates.

554249 None column values
602171 Ability to ignore columns 
726366 allow to ignore specified columns in Assertions 
class 

There are no easy ways to do this in standard DBUnit.

Here are two classes that handles this situation by 
creating a table decorator that hides specified columns 
from visibility.  They are fully documented and are ready 
to drop in as-is to DBUnit or into any project that uses 
DBUnit.

Example usage:

String\[\] excludedColumnNameArray = new String\[\] 
\{"TIMESTAMP", "PRIMARY\_KEY\_ID"\};
ITable actualTable = new ColumnExcludingTable
\(actualDataSet.getTable\("DATABASE\_TABLE"\), 
excludedColumnNameArray\);
ITable expectedTable = new ColumnExcludingTable
\(sendDataSet.getTable\("DATABASE\_TABLE"\), 
excludedColumnNameArray\);

Assertion.assertEquals\(expectedTable, 
actualTable\);


\[Note:  I would have appended this to issue 602171, but 
I don't have the ability to attach files to that case.\]

posts.subject: #25 Xml data set support for Oracle sequence values
posts.author: svandenbussche

posts.text:
Logged In: YES 
user\_id=920526

I've extended FlatXmlDataSet and FlatXmlProducer to allow 
this type of syntax in the XML: 

<dataset>
<SOME\_TABLE ROW\_ID="id:=SEQ\(SOME\_SEQUENCE\)" 
COL1="value1" COL2="value2/>
<SOME\_CHILD\_TABLE FK\_COL="$\{id\}" COL3="value3"/>
</dataset>

The above will generate the next sequence value and assign it 
to ROW\_ID in the first row and at the same time, creates a 
variable named "id" that can be used in subsequent rows.

If you are interested in getting this enhancement, it may help 
you with your request.

tickets.summary: Filtering and ordering through XML feeds (sequential columns
tickets.description:
In a project Im working on we have tweaked the DBUnit
XML expected feeds slightly to address two issues with
the database comparison:

1\. Some columns have incremental \(id\) values and should
be excluded from the expected comparison.
2\. For DB tables that do not define primary keys, the
XML expected data set has to define virtual keys for
sorting.

Our current solution uses a modified XML dataset DTD:

<code>
<\!ELEMENT dataset \(table+\)>
<\!ELEMENT table \(column\*, row\*\)>
<\!ATTLIST table
	name CDATA \#REQUIRED
>
<\!ELEMENT column \(\#PCDATA\)>
<\!ATTLIST column
	filter \(true | false\) \#IMPLIED
	key \(true | false\) \#IMPLIED
>
<\!ELEMENT row \(value | null | none\)\*>
<\!ELEMENT value \(\#PCDATA\)>
<\!ELEMENT null EMPTY>
</code>

Here the column element has optional attributes filter
and key to address two issues above. The expected data
feeds are then fed through a custom made reader class
which stores the columns. Finally these columns are
used by the DBUnit mechanisms and
DefaultColumnFilter.excludedColumnsTable and
DatabaseConnection.getConfig\(\).setProperty\(DatabaseConfig.PROPERTY\_PRIMARY\_KEY\_FILTER,
\_ \).

Obviously, this has taken quite a bit of custom tweaks
of the DBUnit framework, and it would be neat if some
of could be taken care in the package. Our solution
might not be the best, however the issues are clearly
not uncommon. 

For a different approach to more or less the same, see
Steven Caswells post <href
a=http://sourceforge.net/tracker/index.php?func=detail&aid=979764&group\_id=47439&atid=449494>979764</a>.
It should be noted, that although with this solution
the sequential column does not have to be ignored, it
does not address how to specify at what number the
sequence starts. In some cases, as we have found, it is
almost impossible to know before the output data is
generated.

posts.subject: #34 Change private fields to protected
posts.author: rlogiacco

tickets.summary: Change private fields to protected
tickets.description:
I have just extended the Excel and XML data set
functionality so that bold and italic fonts in XLS
input data can control the attributes mentioned in the
1077598
\(http://sourceforge.net/tracker/index.php?func=detail&aid=1077598&group\_id=47439&atid=449494\)
request in the XML output data. \(Should anybody find
this interesting, feel free to ask for guidance\).

The implementation was straight forward, including an
extension of the org.dbunit.dataset.Column class, and
modifications to the XlsDataSet, XlsTable and
XmlDataSetWriter.

The problem is that all these classes \(and any of the
other DBUnit classes Ive looked at\) have several
private fields without any access methods. This
includes constants and writer and meta data objects in
the XmlDataSetWriter, and similar fields in other
DBUnit classes.

My request is that these fields are either set to have
the protected access scope, or that get-methods are
provided for extending the framework. Considering the
nature of the DBUnit project, it seems only natural to
allow for easy and flexible extension, rather than
restricting users.

ist set to 'UTF-8' . This causes problems in languages
like German, French etc .

posts.subject: #40 Allow SQL functions and executes them via xml import in db.
posts.author: kunugiyama

tickets.summary: Allow SQL functions and executes them via xml import in db.
tickets.description:
Not sure if this has been raised or addressed already
\(I looked through the RFE / Bugs section and didn't see
it\).
It would be nice to be able to auto-generate particular
data when loading the XML data into the database. For
example, when performing date sensitive tests, the test
may succeed today or this month only, since the XML
data is hard-coded. Running the test may fail tomorrow
or the next month depending on the type of the test.
For what its worth, the solution I came up with is to
allow SQL functions such as MONTH\(NOW\(\)\), YEAR\(NOW\(\)\),
etc. to be executed during the data upload into the
database.
I have attached a diff file on how I addressed this
functionality.
Quick instructions on the use of this functionality is
simple. Simply pad your SQL function with the delimeter
\(I have chosen "\#\#\#"\) and dbunit SQL will take care of
the rest. For example, \#\#\#MONTH\(NOW\(\)\)\#\#\# = the int
value of the current month.

posts.author: slecallonnec

tickets.summary: Patch to Ant Task to Wrap Operation in a Transaction
tickets.description:
This patch modfies the Operation tag in the Ant task to
allow a new parameter: transaction="true"

This will cause the Operation to be decorated into a
TransactionOperation and thus cause the operation to be
performed as a single transaction.  This allows the
operation to be atomic and to complete much faster on
many databases.

tickets.summary: QuerySet docs
tickets.description:
On Saturday, Oct 2, 2004, at 11:12 America/New\_York, Lenny 
Marks wrote:

Hi Federico,

I have a few more things related to the queryset stuff from last week 
which should be attached as a patch below. First, I added 
documentation and examples to the anttask.xml xdoc for usage of 
queryset. Also, I did a bit of re-factoring on the QuerySet stuff.

I was able remove manual propagation of the Project by making 
AbstractStep extend from org.apache.tools.ant.ProjectComponent. 
This way Ant automatically takes care of setting up the context 
using reflection.

I added an antlib definition for the dbunit ant tasks. \(http://
ant.apache.org/manual/CoreTypes/antlib.html \) This can be used 
with ant version > 1.6. It just simplifies things a bit.

<typedef classpath="$\{dbunit.jar\}"  resource="org/dbunit/ant/
antlib.xml">

instead of:

<taskdef name="dbunit" classpath="$\{dbunit.jar\}"
classname="org.dbunit.ant.DbUnitTask" loaderref="dbunit"/>
<typedef name="dbunit" classpath="$\{dbunit.jar\}"
classname="org.dbunit.ant.QuerySet" loaderref="dbunit"/>

Since queryset can be defined as a reference outside of a dbunit 
task, it must have its own typedef, and the loaderref attribute must 
be specified to ensure that the classes are loaded into same JVM. If 
you forget to specify the loaderref, you get ugly 
ClassCastExceptions.

The only thing was that the row for queryset in the generated 
anttask.html did not have the alternating color when I viewed it. I'm 
assuming that has something to do with style sheets or XSLT, but 
I'm not familiar with that stuff.

Thanks,
Lenny Marks
American Physical Society

tickets.summary: Accept that "" and NULL are the same on Oracle
tickets.description:
We have input/output datasets \(as XML\) which we're
using to validate stored procs against both SQL Server
and Oracle.

When the result on SQL Server is an empty string, it's
null on Oracle. Unfortunately, I can't see any way of
representing both of these states of affairs in an XML
file.

It would be really helpful if there were some way of
either making the Oracle connection automatically
replace nulls with empty strings \(optionally, of
course\) or provide a flag to the dataset comparison to
make it allow nulls and empty values to be compared as
equal.

tickets.summary: When a table has no primary key, use all columns for sorting
tickets.description:
We have some "bag" tables - there is no primary key for
these tables at all.

In order to correctly compare datasets, we need a
reliable sort order. Just using all columns as sort
columns works fine, and is a simple change to
DatabaseDataSet.java. Just insert:

// If there isn't a primary key, sort by all
columns
if \(primaryKeys.length==0\)
\{
primaryKeys=columns;
\}

into getSelectStatement\(\).

That's what we've got in our local copy of DbUnit now,
but it would be nice to have in the main line too :\) Of
course, I could see that this should probably be
configurable...

posts.author: gommma

tickets.summary: Configuration for size of statement batches
tickets.description:
As dbunit is so handy for import/export, we're using it
for large tests as well as system test.

By using streaming datasets, we've had no problem
exporting the data. However, importing it using batched
statements, the system blows up with an
OutOfMemoryException.

Turning off batched statements would, I believe \(but
haven't tested, admittedly\) slow things down hugely. A
good intermediate state, however, would be to use
batched statements but only batch a certain number.

For the moment I've got a temporary hack to the source
to always execute the statement after 100 operations,
but putting this in place properly with a configuration
option would be nicer.

\(I can attempt a patch if that would help.\)

CSV isn't a well defined or standard format, so there's nothing to define it but conventions.  I haven't seen a convention for using comments in CSV files, but that wouldn't prevent you from creating your own.  Since the major advantage of dbunit's CSV support is to provide basic editing in Excel, I would look at how well Excel would support a comment character in one row of data.  

Since I submitted this, I've abandoned CSV files in DBUnit because of the lack of null-value support.  I don't use the XML formats either, but that's another story.

tickets.summary: [PATCH] XMLType Support for Oracle - Again
tickets.description:
Well it looks like someone beat me to it, but I didn't
check these lists until AFTER I'd done it myself. I've
added XMLType support to dbunit for oracle. My way is a
different approach though, so I thought I'd post my
files anyway.

There's only two:
org\dbunit\ext\oracle\OracleXMLTypeDataType.java \(new file\)
org\dbunit\ext\oracle\OracleDataTypeFactory.java
\(replacement file\)

If you download these files, put them in an appropriate
place \(underneath the java dir\) and rebuild then you
should have support for XMLTypes. See the FAQ for
information on how to use oracle data types.

The XMLTypes are stored as binary data in the xml flat
files produced.

Hi aalmiray,

Your class seems very interesting and it would be a great addition for dbunit 
2.2.

However, I'd like to ask a few improvements \(not in the class itself, but in the 
'infrastructure\):

\- could you refactor the test case to not use Spring? I know Spring is great 
and it facilitate the development, but in this case it would get in the way, as it 
is not used in the current tests and hence can cause confusion on future 
maintenance

\- the current DatabaseTestCase could be refactor to delegate it's job to the 
new class - this way, we would have a better reuse. More than that, you could 
create an IDatabaseTester interface \(implemented by DatabaseTester\), which 
in turn would be a parameter for the DatabaseTest constructors \(of course, 
the current constructors would use DatabaseTester to keep compatibility\).

\- it would also be important to update the docs as well, otherwise your 
efforts could be wasted \(in other words, it's necessary to spread the word 
about this enhancement ;-\). In fact, you could use the Spring integration in 
the documentation as well.

\- finally, could you provide a cvs-style patch \(for instance, created from 'cvs 
diff -u' or with Eclipse\) of these changes?

Regards,

\-- Felipe

posts.text:
Logged In: YES 
user\_id=1192026

I've been trying to access de cvs server today but it keeps
failing with a timeout. I'll try tomorrow from another computer.

I have stumbled upon a design issue; if DatabaseTestcase
uses a DatabaseTester behind the scenes the following
changes must happen:

1 mark getConnection as deprecated
2 add protected abstract DataSource getDataSource\(\)
3 add protected IDatabaseTester getDatabaseTester\(\) \{ return
new DatabaseTester\(\); \}
4 in setUp\(\) wire IDatabaseTester and Datasource before any
operations

In that way DatabaseTestcase subclasses can use the default
DatabaseTester or provide ther own, but adds a new method
that will break backward compatibility.

Another aproach would be to create a parallel
DatabaseTestcase with this code, and deal with the backward
imcompatibility in a later release.

\- Andres
posts.subject: #54 Enable TestCase compositions
posts.author: aalmiray

posts.text:

one \(dbunit.cvs.sourceforge.net\) - SF has changed the CVS setup a couple of 
weeks ago and dbunit's site does not have the current information \(sorry\).

Regarding the design issues, we cannot count with a DataSource - what if the 
users are using 'traditional' JDBC connections, obtained through 
DriverManager? So, I would take the route where the DatabaseTester is an 
abstract class with an abstract getConnection\(\) method; you could then 
extend it by a DSDatabaseTester, which would get the connection through 
DataSource \(you could even go one step further and also provide a 
NamedDSDatabaseTester, which would take the DataSource from JNDI\).

So, answering your questions:

> 1 mark getConnection as deprecated

Nope, it would simply call dbTester.getConnection\(\)

> 2 add protected abstract DataSource getDataSource\(\)

DataSource would be a specific characteristic of the DatabaseTester sub-
class; DatabaseTestCase has nothing to do with it \(although it could have a 
sub-class to do so\).

> 3 add protected IDatabaseTester getDatabaseTester\(\) \{ return
>new DatabaseTester\(\); \}

Yep.

>4 in setUp\(\) wire IDatabaseTester and Datasource before any
>operations
I don't think it's necessary - you should just use getDatabaseTester\(\) 
everywhere it's needed. What setUp could do is to call a protected method 
that checks if getDatabaseTeste\(\) does not return null.

I've followed your suggestions and I coded the following:
IDatabaseTester
\\-> AbstractDatabaseTester
|-> DMDatabaseTester \(DriverManager\)
|-> EnvDMDatabaseTester \(DriverManager\)
|-> DSDatabaseTester \(Datasource\)
|-> JndiDSDatabaseTester \(JNDI Datasource\)

There are also 3 subclases of DatabaseTestcase, 1 for each
DatabaseTester, the original DatabaseTestcase uses
EnvDMDatabaseTester as default, it pulls the values using
System.getProperty\(\). In the tests, the values are taken
from the DatabaseEnvironment.

Regarding the Datasource tests, a quick way to create
DataSources \(AFAIK\) is using commons-dbcp, but that would
add 3 more dependencies to the project \(commons-dbcp,
commons-pool and commons-collection\); what are your thoughts
on this matter ?

\- Andres

tickets.summary: Enable TestCase compositions
tickets.description:
Althogh DbUnit is a tool to test database access and
components closely related to the database \(i.e, DAO
layer\) it is also good at establishing a very specific
database state for integration testing. The drawback is
that each integration TestCase must extend
DatabaseTestCase in order to harness that
functionality. What then, if the integration TestCase
has already another class hierarchy ?

The solution would be to create a DatabaseTester \(like
WebTester on jWebUnit\).

I have already coded such solution and used it in
conjuction with the depedencyInjection testCases
bundled with Spring \(tested against 1.2.6 and 2.0M1\)
and would like to contribute it.

posts.subject: #56 Expanded Refresh Operations
posts.author: rlogiacco

tickets.summary: Expanded Refresh Operations
tickets.description:
I've been using DBUnit to manage the reference data for
our integration/development database and it's been very
useful thus far.  However I have some ideas that may
make it even more useful.  \(I've started to implement
some of them, but I wanted to capture them so others
can comment on it\)

First of all, I've written my own foreign key database
filter since the default was too slow.  \(There may have
been some improvements in cvs, but the version I was
using was really slow\)  Basically I get all the foreign
keys at once since it seems to take the same amount of
time to get all vs. get a single one on the database
I'm working with.  That solves part of my problem.

The next issue I've faced is data failing when trying
to refresh it.  The 2 main scenarios are: unique
constraint violations and foreign key violations \(even
with FK filters, you still run into situations where
you're missing necessary data to satisfy FK's\)  My
proposed solution for the unique index issue is to
create an operation that will delete offending data
based on indices for the record you are inserting.  For
the FK issues I've faces, I'd like to insert the
necessary data \(i.e. create random records\) to satisfy
the foreign key constraints.

My ultimate goal is to be able to insert any data into
any database and just have it work with minimal impact
on the existing data.  Obiously this wouldn't be
something for production data, but for JUnits and
integration testing database, this seems reasonable to
me.  I've begun work on some of this, but don't really
have the time to implement all of this.

I appreciate any comments, for or against this proposed
operation.
Tom

posts.author: bascan

tickets.summary: [PATCH] Support for MySQL 5.0 boolean datatype
tickets.description:
Problem:
When a MySQL 5.0 export is performed and a column of
the type bit\(1\) is encountered than the following
method invocation occurs:
IDataTypeFactory.createDatatype\(1111, "bit"\). This
datatype is currently not recognized and therefore the
export fails.

Solution:
The attached patch adds recognition of this datatype to
MySqlDataTypeFactory.createDataType\(int, String\).

Don't forget to set the correct datatype factory with
the following property:
datatypeFactory="org.dbunit.ext.mysql.MySqlDataTypeFactory"

Bas Cancrinus
www.iprofs.nl

I could not find a way to attach this patch to feature

for it.

The patch includes configuration for the Maven 2
changes plugin.

There are three things to note:

1\. The Maven 2 changes plugin does not handle html code
within an action, so some actions look weird.

2\. Because of the way SourceForge constructs the URLs
to the tracker, some URLs to issues goes to an error
page that includes a link to the correct page. This is
because bugs and feature requests use a different
"atid" in their URLs.

3\. Ideally the changes.xml file should be moved out of
the xdoc directory. As it is now a changes.html file is
created by the site plugin for this file. Moving the
changes.xml file to its default location
$\{basedir\}/src/changes/changes.xml and changing the pom
accordingly solves this issue.

This request came from the need for the test setup to create objects from dbUnit data.  dbUnit makes ITables from 2 main sources: the database and the data files \(e.g. flat xml\).  We could have reused production code that created objects from dbUnit ResultSets if dbUnit ITable supported that.

"Testing the database" is one use case - we have many unit tests that test the database, but that is only part of the testing use of dbUnit.

"Testing with the database" is probably more than half of what our tests are - we have lots of automated functional tests that test scenarios with the database, vs mocking it.  This use case is the source of the request.

Using dbUnit for automated functional tests is the use case - something needed to inflate objects from test seed data, not just leave them in ITables.  dbUnit is a good, easy way to do so; even easier if ITable supported ResultSet.

posts.author: gommma

tickets.summary: [PATCH] ReplacementTable - allow fail if value not replaced
tickets.description:
I would like DbUnit to allow a fail-fast when a value is marked \(delimited\) for replacement, but no replacement is found.  For example, if I have a value "$\{test-id\}-1" that should get a replacement, but I accidentally added a replacement for "Test-Id" \(instead of "test-id"\) then the value won't get replaced, when I'm expecting it to.  The reason for the fail-fast is it makes debugging much easier - the developer can immediately fix the test data, rather than debugging his own code that he thinks is failing.

The solution I put together is very simple, and is backwards compatible.  I just added a new property "strictReplacement" to the ReplacementTable and ReplacementDataSet classes.  When set to true \(default is false\), the fail-fast behavior is used.

The patch I submitted includes a test case which I added to ReplacementTableTest.

tickets.summary: Document whitespace handling in XML serialization
tickets.description:
By editing and reformating the XML files produced by dbunit, it is possible that whitespace at the beginning and at the end of strings is introduced.

These mistakes can be a problem, for example I've been locked out of a webapp because the password hash in the bootstrap data now had whitespace around it.

dbunit should make clear whether it considers xml whitespace to be of the significant kind, or not significant. For columns where it is significant, embedding it into a CDATA could be nice. A comment at the top of files serialized by unitdb would also be helpful.

tickets.summary: Public constructors for default data type
tickets.description:
Only a few default data types provide public constructors \(the others are package protected\).  
Is it possible to generalize this characteristic to all data type? 
That would allow more flexibility and ease to overload existing data types.
Example: to overload the DateDataType class, I had to create a PublicDateDataType in the same package, with a public constructor. Then I overloaded this class to create my own type.

posts.subject: #76 Add DatabaseConnection methods to support PreparedStatements
posts.author: sf-robot

tickets.summary: Add DatabaseConnection methods to support PreparedStatements
tickets.description:
Basically for us the change is needed to setup for testing a batch job's "fetcher SQL" - proving that its query works correctly.  The core issue is the use of prepared statements and their parameters.  dbUnit currently does not support that; it only supports SQL strings.

We subclassed DatabaseConnection and added the two methods in the attached file to support testing like this:

String sql = batch.getFetcherSql\(\);

DatabaseConnectionCustom conn = \(DatabaseConnectionCustom\) getConnection\(\);
PreparedStatement stmt = conn.createPreparedStatement\(sql\);
batch.setFetcherSQLParameters\(stmt\);
ITable actualTable = conn.createQueryTable\("Generated\_SQL", stmt\);


The general explanation of the above code is:
1\. Get the fetcher SQL from the batch job class
2\. Create a prepared statement using the dbUnit connection with the SQL
\(using the new createPreparedStatement\)
3\. Pass the statement to the batch class for it to set the parameters.
4\. Execute the prepared statement query \(using the new createQueryTable\).
5\. The rest would be normal dbUnit stuff...


In summary, we can't do those tests without those methods.  We can keep our
custom implementation, of course.  However I think dbUnit should support
prepared statements too, and wonder that others could use this feature.

tickets.summary: Support RowFilter on ITable
tickets.description:
It would be quite useful to have a common usable row filter \(based on arbitrary columns\) for the ITable. So you would be able to load a whole bunch of data from the db into memory and could filter those out that are not relevant for the assertion you want to do currently.

There is already such an implementation which unfortunately only works for primary keys \(org/dbunit/database/PrimaryKeyFilteredTableWrapper.java\).

I appended two proposals based on the PKFilteredTableWrapper:

1\.
RowFilterTableBySingleColumn.java
-> convenient way if you only want to filter rows using a single column

2\.
RowFilterTableByMultipleColumns.java
IRowValueProvider.java
-> to filter rows using arbitrary columns


I would appreciate any comments on this and - of course - if this would finally be included into a release :-\)

Regards,
mat

I suggest to change lines like

logger.debug\("assertValidRowIndex\(row=\{\}\) - start", Integer.toString\(row\)\);

to

tickets.summary: support for tolerated delta in floating-point comparisons
tickets.description:
This is what JUnit provides for floating-point operations by method
assertEquals\(double val1, double val2, double delta\)

I added a patch as a first proposal. By implementing the new interface IDataTypeFactory2 you can create instances of DataType for specific table.column values.
Using the NumberTolerantDataType object provides the comparison methods needed for the DBUnit Assertion then. As an example on how to use it I also added a sample implementation of IDataTypeFactory2 called DeltaDataTypeFactory.

I introduced the new interface IDataTypeFactory2 so that the old API is not broken.

If this proposal makes sense to some of you we should discuss if the existing implementations of the original IDataTypeFactory should also implement the interface IDataTypeFactory2.

Looking forward to your comments.

Thanks and regards,
mat

posts.author: gommma

tickets.summary: Assertion.equals to provide more info about failure
tickets.description:
We compare two tables from two different database schemas with each other having several thousands of rows in each. In order to make the dbunit-assertion work we use the SortedTable which reorders the result rows. 
If one cell does not match dbunit writes a beautiful assertion that only contains the in-memory row number for which the comparison failed. Here an example \(row=1 identifies the in-memory row index\):

junit.framework.AssertionFailedError: value \(table=TEST\_TABLE, row=1, col=COLUMN2\): expected:<row 1 col 2> but was:<wrong value>

Using this ID it is very painful to detect the physical database rows \(expected,actual\) for which the comparison failed. I suggest to introduce an equals method that allows the user to define additional columns to be reported when a comparison fails. These can be used to identify the rows easily on the DB.

The method could look as follows:

Assertion.assertEquals\(ITable expectedTable, ITable actualTable, Column\[\] additionalColumnInfo\)

The failure message could look as follows:

junit.framework.AssertionFailedError: value \(table=TEST\_TABLE, row=1, col=COLUMN2\): expected:<row 1 col 2> but was:<wrong value>
Additional row info: \(col 'MY\_PK\_COLUMN' values: expected=<4728374>, actual=<4728375>\)

Having the additional information about the PK-column it is possible to identify the rows that differ very easily.

The patch including a UnitTest is appended.

Since this change is a very small I would be happy if you could introduce it in the near future.

Thanks in advance and regards,
mat

posts.author: gommma

posts.text:
Logged In: YES 
user\_id=1232035
Originator: NO

-1 for me.

I don't think this is in DbUnit scope.  This type of requirements opens a whole lot of issues that are not DbUnit's responsibility and IMO, shouldn't be as a unit test library -- like exporting from one Rdbms to import into another \(I'm thinking of Oracle to Db2, for example\), etc.  Having dealt with sql exports and cross-database issues in the past, I know this is particularly tricky stuff, and very hard to maintain too because it requires skills in the various RDMSes.  Come to think of it, most issues we have in DbUnit today are to do with the db-specific part...

The original choice of using XML as the primary format was just to avoid this type of concerns which, again, I think are out of the scope of a unit test library.
posts.subject: #82 Add SQL export to file property without using P6Spy in M2
posts.author: slecallonnec

posts.text:
Logged In: YES 
user\_id=1803108
Originator: NO

I partially agree with you. But as long as nobody expects dbunit to do some work on the exported SQL file I think it still is in the scope. 
On the one hand it might be useful for debugging purpose to check if escaping etc. is handled by dbunit correctly \(Imagine to configure PrintStream/OutputStream where all issued SQLs are printed to, somewhat like "hibernate show\_sql", if this can be compared at all\). 
On the other hand \(and this is what you are focusing\) it can be "abused" as kind of an export - but therefore I'd say dbunit would not have to guarantee anything. I know that export/import of db data is very RDMS specific and should not at all be handled by dbunit.

>>Come to think of it, most issues we
>>have in DbUnit today are to do with the db-specific part...
This is very true. And it is really a mess...

We wouldn't even have to use P6Spy since the SQL is already there \(see InsertOperation.getOperationData\).

The other thing that Marcello describes in the initial summary is related to import. If I did not get it wrong he just wants dbunit to export the "insert" statements for a later \_manual\_ offline import into his RDBMS. So the import side would not be touched in dbunit - otherwise I'd also say -1 since importing plain SQL data is out of scope in my eyes.

Marcello, could you please clarify this? Did you mean dbunit to export \_and\_ import plain SQL files or do you just like to have the exported "insert" statements?

Logged In: YES 
user\_id=1803108
Originator: YES

Hi Sebastien,

Regarding your 2 comments:

\- "as long as the AbstractTableMetaData is immutable with respect to the
"Column\[\]" object"  What do you mean by that?  Isn't that the point of rev.
643, i.e. modifying Column\[\] as new columns are being discovered \(I
actually hadn't seen that change before\)? -> That is no contradiction since the discovery of new columns is handled by a buffer and the final DatabaseMetaData is created AFTER all Column\[\] information has been gathered - so the Column\[\] array is still final in the resulting DatabaseMetaData \(passed in once in the constructor and never changed later\)
\- Can we use a HashMap rather than Hashtable? -> I don't mind using a HashMap instead. But the Hashtable does not allow null values which I find advantageous in this special case.

If you wish we could continue the conversation on the dev-lists.

Regards,
mat

posts.author: gommma

tickets.summary: Performance optimization getColumnIndex()
tickets.description:
Hi Roberto/Sebastien/community

I had a deeper look into the code with respect to performance. I found that the method AbstractTable.getColumnIndex\(String colName\) is invoked for every ForwardOnlyResultSet.getValue\(\). The implementation of "getColumnIndex" loops every time through all columns to find the index of
the current column. This causes a heavy impact on performance for me since my test table has aprox. 3200 rows.
In the patch I attached I introduced a new method getColumnIndex in the AbstractTableMetaData which is accessiblevia the interface ITableMetaData. It uses a hashtable internally and the performance results are really amazing.
I measured the time that the call "Assertion.assertEquals\(ITable expectedTable, ITable actualTable\)" takes with the following results:

old \(2.2.3\) version:   1130 secs
my patch applied:      299 secs
so the improvement factor is approx. >3.5 which is really significant to me\!

I would like to ask you to review it \(especially Roberto/Sebastien\) and if you agree I would commit it to the repository. 
At this time I do not see any drawbacks of this solution, as long as the AbstractTableMetaData is immutable with respect to the "Column\[\]" objects. The only ugliness is that the new method invokes the "getColumns\(\)" from the interface which is not implemented in the AbstractTableMetaData itself but only by the concrete implementations. But I do not see a problem with that. Please let me know what you think.

Thanks a lot
mat

tickets.summary: Alternative support for CLOB and BLOB in Oracle 11 JDBC
tickets.description:
I have some problems with CLOBs and BLObs while using Oracle JDBC 11.1.0.6 with Oracle Database 10.2.0.4.0.

First I tried to use OraclaDataTypeFactory, but it requires access to Oracle native connection, which was impossible for me, because my connection is wrapped by C3P0, hibernate and spring.

Also I would like not to use oracle.sql.CLOB and BLOB Types \(I use org.hibernate.lob.ClobImpl and BlobImple instead\).

I've read:
\* http://www.oracle.com/technology/sample\_code/tech/java/codesnippet/jdbc/clob10g/handlingclobsinoraclejdbc10g.html 
\* http://jasonbherald.com/2008/05/27/oracle-blob-jdbc-how-to/
and write new DataTypeFactory \(see attachment\).

It uses new ClobAsStringDataType class for handling Clobs using standard jdbc ResultSet.getString and ResultSet.setString methods \(contrary to oracle custom api\).

For Blobs, uses BinaryStreamDataType.

It works great, even for clobs bigger than 1 MB.

Probably it will work with Oracle 10.x JDBC drivers.

posts.text:
P6Spy has a serious bug that causes it to mix up fields that have the same name, e.g. if you join two tables which both have a 'title' column, P6Spy will mix them up and the values end up switched over. 

So I can't \(and many others won't be able\) to use P6Spy to log dbunit SQL statements. 

This bug in P6Spy is in their issue tracking - I saw it earlier this year, however at this point in time, their issue tracking site has lost its domain name. I have no idea if this is just temporary or due to the fact that p6spy has not seen any activity for years. 

So I'd like to vote for this feature request to be given higher priority.

posts.text:
I did the following work on this which is committed in svn rev. 815/trunk:

=> the AmbiguousTableNameException is thrown by the new class "OrderedTableNameMap" which also handles case sensitivity.
=> it is now not possible anymore to create a FlatDtdDataSet/FlatXmlDataSet/DatabaseDataSet/... with two tables having equal names \(fail-fast implemented by "OrderedTableNameMap" class\) - in older versions this was possible and the AmbiguousTableNameException was thrown in "getTable\(\)" invocation on IDataSet
=> removed some of the "testXxxDuplicateTablesXxx\(\)" tests \(e.g. "testDuplicateWrite" from XxxDataSetTest\) since "duplicate-table DataSets cannot be created anymore - central location where this is tested is in AbstractDataSetTest and its implementors during the creation of a DataSet.

posts.subject: #97 Fail-Fast if AmbiguousTableNames found
posts.author: gommma

tickets.summary: Fail-Fast if AmbiguousTableNames found
tickets.description:
As already discussed on the developers list \(around 20. Sept 2008\) it would be nice to have a fail-fast mechanism which throws the AmbiguousTableNameException immediately during the construction of an IDataSet and not only in the invocation of the "IDataSet.getTable\(String tabName\)".
So if you have an IDataSet instance you can always be sure that no duplicate table names are inside.

This is also a preparation for \#1214252 which is about case-sensitive table names \(as they were until 1.5\).

tickets.summary: Additional column info also for filtered columns
tickets.description:
With respect to \#1966517 \(additional column info on failure\) another useful feature popped up. I compare tables which have a surrogate key. The surrogate key is filtered out in the assertions. If a failure occurs it would be helpful to print out the value of this surrogate key so that the failed db-row can be identified by this key. Since the key column is filtered out in the table-metadata it cannot be displayed when passing it to the 

Assertion.assertEquals\(ITable expectedTable, ITable actualTable, Column\[\] additionalColumnInfo\)

method. It should be extended so that filtered columns can also be displayed.

Until now when a \#REQUIRED attribute was found the Column object was set to "Column.NO\_NULLS" which is obviously not the case anymore when I would apply your fix. Do you have an idea for this?

BTW, thanks for your last feedback. It helped me solving the above mentioned issue and made the dbunit API cleaner.

posts.subject: #102 Generated DTD should use #IMPLIED if a column has a default
posts.author: gommma

tickets.summary: Generated DTD should use #IMPLIED if a column has a default
tickets.description:
Most of the tables in our \(Sybase\) database include a couple of audit columns, Last\_Updated\_At & Last\_Updated\_By, which store the timestamp and user's login ID when rows are inserted or updated.  Because these are NOT NULL columns, the generated DTD had CDATA \#REQUIRED for all these attributes.  However, the columns have defaults bound to them \(using getdate\(\) and suser\_name\(\) respectively\) so values don't need to be supplied for these when inserting rows and \#IMPLIED would therefore be more appropriate.
The DatabaseMetaData.getColumns\(\) javadocs include "COLUMN\_DEF String => default value \(may be null\)" in the returned column description, which I'm guessing could be used to determine this?

posts.subject: #108 Bundle required slf4j components with dbunit
posts.author: sf-robot

tickets.summary: Bundle required slf4j components with dbunit
tickets.description:
Per the DBUnit Wiki: "As of version 2.2.1, DbUnit uses the SLF4J library for logging purposes \(See dependencies\). This allows DbUnit users to use their favourite logging library \(log4j, JCL, etc.\).

When using DbUnit, you must therefore download SLF4J and include slf4j-api-\*.jar and the implementation library of your choice in the classpath: for example, slf4j-log4j12-\*.jar for Log4J, or slf4j-jcl-\*.jar for commons-logging. However, make sure there is only one implementation library in your classpath, as failing to do so might cause some issues."

It seems like the more user friendly route would be to pick  logging library \(I vote for commons logging\) and then provide instructions on how to adapt for others.  Alternatively, maybe bundle all of slf4j and place somewhere outside of the classpath.  This would make it easier for users to switch to a different logging library since all the needed slf4j jars would be distributed with DBUnit.

tickets.summary: Include table name in cyclic dependency exception
tickets.description:
In DatabaseSequenceFilter$DependencyInfo.checkCycles, the CyclicTablesDependencyException is thrown with a message that contains the problem dependent tables.  Add DependencyInfo.tableName to this message to make tracking down cyclic problems easier.

This problem arose as I was trying to export an XSD of my table metadata.  I  systematically excluded the tables that had cyclic dependencies but I needed to debug to get the name of the table to exclude \(aka DepedencyInfo.tableName\).  Excluding only the listed dependent tables did not solve the problem.

tickets.description:

http://srivaths.blogspot.com/2007/04/dbunit-sequence-numbers-foreign-keys.html

dbunit does currently not support the creation of db-relationships based on db-sequence values.

It would be nice to have some kind of placeholder for sequence values. Also it must be possible to link the corresponding table entries. \(maybe "Statement\#getGeneratedKeys\(\)" can be used therefore???\)

Hey Jason, Roberto,

posts.subject: #120 Support schema and table create / drop
posts.author: gommma

posts.text:
A separate module, that's an idea.  I was actually thinking of introducing something like the Hibernate Dialects into dbunit \(part of it's already there in the form of data types, but there's no SQL for specific databases\) and determining the database at runtime off of the metadata \(and ansi compliance if it's an unsupported dialect, at least we can do ansi sql, or throw an exception if neither of those two work\) from the JDBC Connection.  I'd add four new operations \(create schema, create table, drop schema, and drop table\).  The hard part would be either modifying the current XML format or creating a new format for the DDL generation.  If it were a module, would it be a good place to store it within dbunit \(maybe under a contrib, extras, modules folder\) or a new project?

I completely agree about staying backwards compatible, and I don't want to introduce new methods into interfaces or base classes that would mess this up.  Fortunately it looks like the code coverage for the tests is pretty good so they should catch the bulk of things if this were to introduce a change that would affect compatibility.  I saw Jailer, but didn't really look into it much.
posts.subject: #120 Support schema and table create / drop
posts.author: lightguard

posts.text:
At this point in time a separate module would mean to just create a new subpackage, e.g. org.dbunit.ddl.

We are currently discussing the migration of the dbunit-maven-plugin into dbunit \(see \#2215029\) which would then allow us to define modules as separate maven projects with an own pom.xml. In my eyes this would be the cleanest solution. But this is currently future sound...

For your implementation you may also consider to use the dbunit DTD \(or perhaps an XML schema, but this is not supported yet by dbunit\) to retrieve the information you need for the DDL. It becomes a bit more difficult if somebody wants to use the Excel XLS format to create a DDL from...

Ant task Export with ForwardOnlyResultSetTableFactory
tickets.description:
Hello.

I found that around dbunit 2.1/2.2 Ant's Export task was switched from ForwardOnlyResultSetTableFactory to CachedResultSetTableFactory factory. That decision leaded to impossibility export databases that exceed amount of JVM Heap size \(i.e. -Xmx option\) and natively limited by overall RAM size. 
It will be nice if you can restore the functionality of version 2.1 and prior. 

I try to do a patch by myself \(i.e. org.dbunit.ant.getExportDataSet. IDataSet dataset = getDatabaseDataSet\(connection, this.\_tables, true\); true - means use ForwardOnlyResultSetTableFactory instead of CachedResultSetTableFactory. It works nice with "plain" table export, but leads to NPE on Export + Query and fails some tests as well.

Hope the feature will be useful for other users as well.

Thanks.

posts.subject: #137 Separate Maven module for dbunit-oracle
posts.author: jbhurst

posts.text:
We decided that rather than add a module for Oracle, we will include the Oracle JDBC driver as a dependency in the main module. Anyone who wants to build dbunit will need to download this dependency manually and add it to his local Maven repo.

I will raise another ticket to work on the static typing in the Oracle data type support and add instructions for adding the Oracle driver to the local Maven repo.
posts.subject: #137 Separate Maven module for dbunit-oracle
posts.author: jbhurst

tickets.summary: Separate Maven module for dbunit-oracle
tickets.description:
Currently the OracleDataTypeFactory and related classes are coded using reflection, to avoid a compile-time dependency on ojdbc\(14|6\).jar.

Split the Maven project into multiple modules so that Oracle-specific code can be in an optional module. Developers can add the ojdbc jar to their local Maven repos manually if they need to build the Oracle code.

I and Zdenek Vrablik have coded a patch for this. See http://github.com/johnbhurst/dbunit/tree/maven-multimodule/patch for the work in progress. \(You can download the current source tree for my working branch.\)

If this new module structure is accepted into DbUnit, I will rework and extend the OracleDataTypeFactory and related classes.

posts.subject: #138 Add support for dynamic replacements
posts.author: gommma

tickets.summary: Add support for dynamic replacements
tickets.description: I extended the replacement data set to allow for dynamic replacement.  Instead of replacing keys in the data with values from a Map this passes values in the dataset to a function \(registered with that table and column\) and replaces the values with the results of the function.  The function is passed the original value and a reference to the ITable object so it can grab values from other columns.  I use this to replace dates in my dataset with new dates that are timeshifted to new point in time.  Thanks for keeping up the good work. Hope this helps someone else.  I'll upload my code.  It is all new files with no changes to existing files.

posts.subject: #139 Fix "integration tests"
posts.author: jbhurst

posts.text:
I've got the integration tests working again, with Oracle, PostgreSQL, MySQL, HSQLDB, Apache Derby and H2.

There are numerous issues remaining however:
\- Not yet working for MS SQL-Server, Sybase
\- Different database targets not supported by CI build
\- Integration tests are not separated from unit tests; it's not clear whether any given test requires a running database or not
\- DatabaseEnvironment/Profile mechanism is quite awkward for specifying which test/features are to be done for which database targets. Current system uses a string to specify features to skip. Better to specify features to include?
\- SQL DDL not executed by Maven. Confusing/difficult manual steps to set up database schema
\- Some integration tests \(e.g. SQLHelperDomainPostgreSQLTest\) don't play well in current framework. I.e. executes its own DDL in the middle of the test
\- "Fixing" the tests introduced a few ugly hacks \(marked "TODO"\) that need to be cleaned up.

Thus, I am keeping the ticket open for now while I work on these.
posts.subject: #139 Fix "integration tests"
posts.author: jbhurst

posts.text: Jeff converted the properties to maven profiles recently. Apart from that, we've also fixed most of the tests at this time and can run them against Oracle, PostgreSQL, MySQL, HSQLDB, Derby and H2 in custom DbUnit Docker containers. The whole thing is wired up using Concourse CI, however, Concourse CI is still buggy in our test environments, seeking long term solution yet.

posts.subject: #140 Add support for Oracle SDO_GEOMETRY datatype
posts.author: jbhurst

posts.text:
I'll attach a new implementation which has the following changes:

1\. The original implementation used oracle STRUCT objects to read and write sdo\_geometry objects to/from the database.  To do this oracle requires a Connection.  This connection is not available in DataType.typeCast\(Object\) so there were some scenarios were it was impossible to call typeCast.  The new implementation uses oracle jpub to render classes corresponding the the sdo\_geometry related types.  Theses rendered classes have embeded type information and do not require a connection.

2\. Changed string parsing in typeCast to use regular expressions and handle more scenarios.

3\. Added test scenarios for OracleSdoGeometryDataType

4\. Added basic documentation in OracleSdoGeometryDataType.java on how to use jpub to render other oracle objects into classes for use with dbunit.

5\. StringDataType.typeCast was changed to call toString for unknown object types.  This is required for custom objects that do not fit the normal java.sql.\* datatypes.  Test cases for StringDataType were adjusted accordingly.

tickets.summary: Replace reflection-based Oracle data types with static calls
tickets.description:
The current Oracle data type support uses awkward and error-prone reflection to call Oracle-specific classes and methods in the Oracle JDBC driver.

This has been done to avoid a compile-time dependency on the Oracle JDBC JAR, which cannot be obtained automatically from a Maven repository.

The team has decided to make the Oracle JDBC JAR a dependency of the project, and do away with the reflection-based code.

posts.text:
I agree that trace and the entry methods are the better choices.  For tracing, my favorite option is AOP, as it prevents the manual entry of all those method calls where needed \(and when you need them, they are never there\!\) and is very easy to turn on and off.  Of course, this requires an AOP framework, such as AspectJ or Spring AOP...
Additionally, if your app uses one of those, then your app requires no trace logging messages in dbUnit, as your AOP will add them.  In this case, it is best if dbUnit had no trace messages as the debug ones\!

Christopher, it would be great if you do a simple setup and convert a couple of classes and attach the files as a clear exhibit of your preferred suggested approach.
posts.subject: #145 Use SLF4J XLogger for execution tracing
posts.author: jeffjensen

posts.text:
I think two considerations has to be take in account:
1\. dbunit is a unit testing framework, not an application so the usage of AOP for logging could have some cons we need to take in consideration: another runtime dependency, more complex startup even if you only need INFO level \(the default\), etc...
2\. we introduced the logging statements to ease bug identification and resolution, allowing our users to give more detailed informations on what is happening on their machines and why their test are failing unexpectedly, not to print out the statements we are going to run on the database \(P6Spy is one option in this case\) nor to debug your unit tests
3\. the DEBUG level should be disabled by default and enabled only when problems arises INSIDE dbunit, or whenever you think the problem could be INSIDE dbunit, that's why we didn't make all so differences in logging statements: if you want to get our help in solving your problem the more informations we can have the faster we can get to the point.

With all this clear in mind do you really think it's worth to spend all the needed effort to refine all those logging statements \(by the way, I used log4e and I've added all logging with 1 click only...\)? Do you really think it is needed? Consider we have logging in dbunit since 2.1, the framework didn't had logging at all for many years and it has got a lot of attention by the community....

I often think I did a mistake adding the logging statements...
posts.subject: #145 Use SLF4J XLogger for execution tracing
posts.author: rlogiacco

posts.text:
In general, AOP for this kind of tracing stuff is nice, though I agree that's probably too large a hammer to be using on this particular problem.  A nice thing about this approach is that you don't need any new dependencies; you've already got SLF4J, and that's all you need.

I wasn't aware of log4e until now... looks like that probably mitigates concern \#2 to some degree.  Given your description of your intention regarding these logging statements, this issue probably all boils down to personal preferences to some degree.  That being said, I'll see if I can whip up some samples of what I was talking about in the next few days or so and put it out there, as Jeff suggested.

Thanks
posts.subject: #145 Use SLF4J XLogger for execution tracing
posts.author: *anonymous

posts.text:
Sorry, it must not be not clear in my first comments - the AOP influence would be from the application setup/tests using dbUnit, not dbUnit adding something, and mainly for TRACE level logging.  dbUnit need change nothing for users to have AOP handling.

I think the adding of the logging statements is a very good thing, and maybe even more are needed and/or just some adjustments\!

Christopher, your examples will clarify intentions and help influence direction, if any is taken to make changes \(as Roberto mentioned, it takes someone willing to take the time to do the changes.  Which means, as usual, "patches are welcome", but I do hate saying that... ;-\).

posts.subject: #149 Add timezone support for timestamp data type
posts.author: jbhurst

tickets.summary: Add timezone support for timestamp data type
tickets.description:
Request:
Timezone support for the timestamp data type \(and probably for the date data type and the time data type as well\) would be very useful for some DbUnit users.



Analysis:
Currently, timezones are not supported since the parsing effort is being delegated to java.sql.Timestamp.valueOf\(\) which cannot handle timezones. Actually, Timestamp.valueOf\(\) silently uses the default timezone. This can lead to surprising results. Consider the following code and the two different outputs, depending on the default timezone:

System.out.println\( "jvm's timezone: " + TimeZone.getDefault\(\).getID\(\) \);
String input = "2007-12-05 10:00:00.000000000";
Timestamp ts = Timestamp.valueOf\( input \);
System.out.println\( input + "'s  is " + ts.getTime\(\) + " milliseconds since January 1, 1970, 00:00:00 GMT" \);

\- with the default timezone of my system:

jvm's timezone: Europe/Zurich
2007-12-05 10:00:00.000000000 is parsed to be 1196845200000 milliseconds since January 1, 1970, 00:00:00 GMT

\- in UTC:

jvm's timezone: UTC
2007-12-05 10:00:00.000000000 is parsed to be 1196848800000 milliseconds since January 1, 1970, 00:00:00 GMT

\(credit to Guillaume Cottenceau, see http://archives.postgresql.org/pgsql-jdbc/2007-12/msg00014.php\)



Solution:
Use java.text.DateFormat.parse\(\) instead. See attached patch.

posts.text: Thanks\!  Your comments are clear, and would be great if you attached file patch\(es\) for them to save us recreating any of your work.

tickets.summary: General Logging Message Change Requests
tickets.description:
Hi, I work for a company that uses DBunit to test on multiple databases and when we're using it to insert data into various tables for testing purposes, we have always encountered a lot of logging messages that we feel should probably only occur under debug logging, but these always show up under general info logging. I'm fairly new at this also, so please excuse my ignorance if I used a "dumb" fix or it sounds like I don't know what I'm talking about, because I probably don't, lol.

I personally went in and made my own changes, then rebuilt dbunit, and everything works the way we need it to. However, I don't want to contribute this because I'm not sure if everyone would want it this way or not. Thus, I'm just going to list here what I did and if you want to make these changes also you can.

\#1

Action: Changed BytesDataType.class to remove INFO logging and replaced it with DEBUG logging for the warning "Assuming given string to be Base64 and not a URI or File".

Reason: To prevent a warning message from displaying during every instance of an insert statement containing Byte values. This was coming up in our logs during every insert line containing a Byte value, making the log fairly huge and annoying.

\#2

Action: Changed DatabaseTableMetaData.class to comment out a logging message that was set for table name case correction.

Reason: To prevent an info logging from occurring during insert on any table using MySQL as the database. I had to comment this one out entirely for now \(it didn't have a debug/info option\) until we figure out the actual problem. It says it's for table name case correction, but everything looks correct from what we can tell, no case correction should be needed, and we just haven't had time to investigate further.

\#3

Action: Changed DefaultDataTypeFactory.class to include "Teradata", "Adaptive Server Enterprise", and "Netezza NPS" in the DATABASE\_PRODUCTS Collection. 

Reason: To prevent a warning message from displaying during the refresh for every insert into a table in regards to TeraData, Sybase, and Netezza being incompatible with the selected DataTypeFactory. The driver and/or version of the database we use for these must operate differently than the norm, as the naming convention being fetched wasn't matching what already existed in the class file. Thus, I added more. 

So yeah, like I said, I'm not especially savvy when it comes to this stuff, so maybe there were easier ways to get around all of this, but oh well, this is what I did and I'm just letting you all know in case you want to add it or make a better correction for me and include it in the next release.

THANK YOU IN ADVANCE\!\!

The current ReplacementDataSet is too limited. It's too hardcoded around strings and maps of replacements.

More interesting replacements are possible too:
\- Replace value with current time
\- Replace value prefixed "blob:" with BLOB content read from file/URL
\- Replace value prefixed "clob:" with text read from file/URL
\- Replace value prefixed "script:" with result of scripting function

... etc.

tickets.summary: Oracle SDO_GEOMETRY column support
tickets.description:
dbunit version 2.4.7 - Oracle JDBC jar ojdbc14-10.2.0.1.0 

Restoring SDO\_GEOMETRY returns the following stacktrace:

...
Caused by: java.sql.SQLException: Internal Error: Inconsistent catalog view
	at oracle.jdbc.driver.DatabaseError.throwSqlException\(DatabaseError.java:158\)
	at oracle.jdbc.driver.DatabaseError.throwSqlException\(DatabaseError.java:206\)
	at oracle.sql.StructDescriptor.initMetaData1\_9\_0\(StructDescriptor.java:2095\)
	at oracle.sql.StructDescriptor.initMetaData1\(StructDescriptor.java:2034\)
	at oracle.sql.StructDescriptor.isInstantiable\(StructDescriptor.java:1212\)
	at oracle.sql.STRUCT.<init>\(STRUCT.java:142\)
	at org.dbunit.ext.oracle.OracleSdoGeometryDataType.typeCast\(OracleSdoGeometryDataType.java:346\)
	... 7 more

This problem can be fixed by qualifying the SDO\_GEOMETRY when using createDescriptor.

In other words, editing OracleSdoGeometryDataType:343 from:
StructDescriptor geometryDescriptor = StructDescriptor.createDescriptor\(SDO\_GEOMETRY, connection\);
to:
StructDescriptor geometryDescriptor = StructDescriptor.createDescriptor\("MDSYS." + SDO\_GEOMETRY, connection\);

fixes the issue. The other call on line 336 should also be changed, but I do not have a test case for this one.

posts.subject: #154 Plain text data set
posts.author: achelis

tickets.summary: Plain text data set
tickets.description:
It would be nice to have a simpler way of writing datasets and to have datasets in an easier to read format. My personal opinion is that XML files can get a bit bloated at times.

An example syntax could be:
\*\*authors\*\*
id | name
\-----------
0  | Iain M Banks
1  | Stephen King

\*\*books\*\*
id | category
\-------------
0  | Fantasy
1  | Sci-Fi 

While this format will have some restrictions compared to XML - lack of foreign/primary keys etc. I think it can still be useful. The idea is to provide something with a very simple, easy to read syntax, which can be used for the majority of db-tests.

A sample implementation has been attached.

posts.subject: #155 Implement DBUnit connection pooling/caching
posts.author: gerald_quintana

tickets.summary: Implement DBUnit connection pooling/caching
tickets.description:
As described in DBUnit FAQ, to improve performance, it is very important to keep DBUnit connection opened as long as possible to avoid metadata information loading. Yet no class is provided by DBUnit to achieve this.

For a real project I developped a PoolingDatabaseConnectionProvider which I find very useful \(see attached patch\) and brought use to reduce by 30% unit test duration. The main idea is to keep it with a singleton and reuse it in all test methods. See the ProviderDBTestCaseIT to see how it works.

About connection closing, as I keep DBUnit connections opened as long as possible it's difficult to determine when to close them, here are sev- eral ideas:
\- Add a fake EndTestCase in each TestSuite
\- Implement finalize method in provider --> done
\- Register shutdown hook
\- Declare the provider singleton with Spring Fwk, use DI to get it in test case and destroy-method to close connectiosn --> my favorite solution

tickets.summary: Possibility to define QueryTimeout
tickets.description:
We had some trouble on our CI server using DBUnit and Oracle. Someone accidentially locked some tables that get filled by DBUnit.
That caused our build to hang for many hours waiting for the locks to get released.

Usually it is quite easy to set a timeout using Statement.setQueryTimeout\(int\) :
http://download.oracle.com/docs/cd/E17476\_01/javase/1.4.2/docs/api/java/sql/Statement.html\#setQueryTimeout%28int%29 

Since the SQL statements are generated somewhere inside DBUnit it is quite hard to get the plain java.sql.Statement to set this property. I tried to extend the StatementFactory but then I would have to completely re-implement BatchStatement, SimpleStatement and PreparedBatchStatement. This classes do only have a package private constructor that I can't access from my custom StatementFactory or a extended \(Batch\)Statement implementation. \(I could place my classes in a package called org.dbunit.database.statement, but I think this is quite bad style\).

Best regards
Benjamin

tickets.summary: FAQ - external files for blob columns
tickets.description:
It was quite hard to find out how simple it is to use external files for blob values.
You have a entry in your changelog:
===
http://www.dbunit.org/changes-report.html\#a2.1
Ability to import external file for binary data types like BLOB; the value can be either a qualified URL or a file path name.
===
It was not clear to me that you simply can use a absolute or relative path as value. I was searching for how to tell dbunit that the blob-value references an external file. I thought you would have to provide some special syntax until I found the implementation of BytesDataType which made things clear to me.

So please add a simple example to your FAQ stating how to specifiy filenames or urls and that there is a limit of 255 Characters for a URL or filename.

Thanks
Benjamin

posts.author: bluecarat

tickets.summary: Patch: Insert Binary data in VARCHAR and BYTE fields
tickets.description:
We have implemented some code to describe binary data for BYTE and VARCHAR. This is useful for siutations in which you want to store non readable characters in fields with these types.

One example would be binary coded decimal \(BCD\) fields in CHAR FOR BITDATA columns which is e.g. supported by DB2.

Currently it only supports values up to 64 Bits and uses a hardcoded prefix to distinguish a normal String from a String with a binary sequence.

A patch with the change and corresponding tests is appendet.

Cheers,
Chris Stahlhut for the BLUECARAT developement team.

posts.author: jeffjensen

tickets.summary: Reduce FlatXmlProducer memory footprint
tickets.description:
I use DbUnit for loading large flat datasets.
Memory footprint is very high because FlatXmlDataSet is a CachedDataSet and it keep all the data in memory.
The problem is that FlatXmlProducer instanciate Columns directly from SAX output, so attribute names and values all always new Strings, even though they might be equal to previously seen data \(column names, foreign keys\).

Why not use a cache \(this behavior could be optional\) that would reuse String instances?

I can provide a patch if you're interested.

Sincerely,

Stéphane Landelle

posts.subject: #173 Few remarks about dbunit
posts.author: jeffjensen

tickets.summary: Few remarks about dbunit
tickets.description:
Hello. I've been using dbunit from three days for my own purpose, which is moving the configuration tables between the test and production environment, and I have few remarks to share about this tool. First one is that this is really cool and I've finally achieved my goal on this, thanks\!

The remarks:
1\. The operation classes design is really ugly, with package method visibility. You cannot use this code to derive your classes, to extend base functionality. I'd recommend to redesign this and allow people to make subclasses with own code \(currently you cannot do anything but copy a lot of classes code into your application, and change it\).
2\. The restriction of working with primary key tables only is easy to overcome, in such scenario you can treat all columns as primary keys, and make update of all columns with \(also\) all columns-where. This works fine for me.
3\. The FlatXmlDataset has a bug, or I did something badly. The scenario:
\* export table A with some column X
\* first exported row has X=null, so it's not contained in Flat XML
\* second one has X=1, and is contained in Flat XML
\* on the import, the importer \(probably\) analyses the first row, and decides that the table A doesn't have X column \(because first row doesn't have it\), so the import of the second row doesn't consider importing of X=1 column

tickets.summary: Accept column names including the character #
tickets.description:
Hello all,

We've come across an old legacy database where some of the columns are named for instance EDL\#DTTRS or ESO\#NMDBT. 
We are using Hibernate as an ORM. We have created DAOs for our entities and created integration tests connecting directly to the "real" test database which worked fine.
When I tried to create a unit test for my DAO with a local in memory \(HSQLDB\) database, using the @DataSet of Unitils \(and my custom DataSetFactory using the XmlDataSet instead of the FlatXmlDataSet\), I got the "table not found" error for my entities that had columns with the \# sign in them.

After some debugging, I found out that the initialize method of the DatabaseDataSet class find all the tables defined in my entities, but the only 2 that have those kinds of column names.
Could it be possible for you guys to do something about this?

Using latest version of everything \(Hibernate 4.1.7.Final, DBUnit 2.4.9, JUnit 4.10\).

Thanks a bunch in advance

posts.text:
Ah, the XML tags got stripped out. Sigh. Anyway, the proposal for the syntax was:

<some_table uuid="uuid'029afe42-d8e7-11e2-aca1-50e549c9b654'" another_column="blah"/>
